title: Git: Building An SQL Reference - Quiz
description: Test your understanding of Git version control including commits, branches, merging, and common workflows

questions.question: What does `git init` do?

options.text: Clones a remote repository to your local machine
Creates a new Git repository by initializing a .git directory
Installs Git on your system
Resets an existing repository to its initial state

questions.explanation: The 'git init' command initializes a new Git repository by creating a hidden .git directory in the current folder. This directory contains all the metadata and object database needed for version control.

questions.question: What is the purpose of `git add .`?

options.text: Commits all changes to the repository
Stages all new and modified files in the current directory and subdirectories for the next commit
Adds a new remote repository
Creates a new file called '.' in the repository

questions.explanation: The 'git add .' stages all new and modified files (in the current directory and below) for the next commit. The dot (.) refers to the current directory. This moves changes to the staging area (index) but does not create a commit.

questions.question: What does `git log --oneline` display?

options.text: Full commit details including author, date, and diff
A condensed log with abbreviated commit hash and commit message on one line each
Only the first commit in the repository
The log of the most recent commit only

questions.explanation: The --oneline flag shows a compact log format: each commit is displayed on a single line with its abbreviated hash and commit message. This is useful for quickly scanning the commit history.

questions.question: How do you create and switch to a new branch called 'feature' in one command?

options.text: git branch feature && git switch feature
git checkout -b feature
git new-branch feature
git create feature --switch

questions.explanation: The 'git checkout -b feature' command creates a new branch called 'feature' and immediately switches to it. The modern equivalent is 'git switch -c feature'. Option A works but uses two commands, not one.

questions.question: What does the `.gitignore` file do?

options.text: Deletes files that match the specified patterns
Hides files from the file system
Specifies files and patterns that Git should not track or stage
Prevents specific users from accessing the repository

questions.explanation: The .gitignore file tells Git which files or directories to ignore (not track). Files matching patterns in .gitignore won't appear in 'git status' as untracked and won't be staged by 'git add'. Common entries include node_modules/, *.log, and .env.

questions.question: What does `git merge feature` do when run from the `main` branch?

options.text: Merges main into the feature branch
Merges the feature branch into the current branch (main)
Deletes the feature branch
Creates a new branch from feature and main combined

questions.explanation: When you run 'git merge feature' while on the main branch, it merges the changes from the feature branch into your current branch (main). The feature branch itself is not modified or deleted.

questions.question: What does `git diff` show?

options.text: The difference between the remote and local repository
Changes in the working directory that have not been staged
A list of all branches and their differences
The difference between the first and last commit

questions.explanation: Running 'git diff' with no arguments shows the changes in your working directory that have not yet been staged (added to the index). To see staged changes, use 'git diff --staged' (or --cached).

questions.question: What does `git stash` do?

options.text: Permanently deletes uncommitted changes
Temporarily saves uncommitted changes and reverts the working directory to a clean state
Creates a new branch with the current changes
Commits changes with a default message

questions.explanation: The 'git stash' command temporarily shelves (stashes) changes you've made to your working directory so you can work on something else. You can reapply stashed changes later with 'git stash pop' or 'git stash apply'.

questions.question: What is the staging area (index) in Git?

options.text: A remote server where code is deployed
An intermediate area where changes are prepared before committing
A backup copy of the repository
The area where deleted files are temporarily stored

questions.explanation: The staging area (also called the index) is an intermediate area between your working directory and the repository. When you 'git add' files, they move to the staging area. When you 'git commit', the staged changes are saved as a commit.

questions.question: Which command shows all branches in a Git repository, including remote branches?

options.text: git branch
git branch -a
git branch --list
git show-branches

questions.explanation: The 'git branch -a' (or --all) command lists all branches, both local and remote-tracking branches. Without flags, 'git branch' shows only local branches. The -r flag shows only remote-tracking branches.
